It's very regular for them to drive two hours just to get to a destination or a location. So I'm originally from, well, I was born in Africa, but spent all my life in Seattle, Washington. And I was a regional vice president of healthcare new sales over there. And then I left in the first initial layoffs back in 2022 and decided, hey, you know what? I've been working for so long. I've had these golden hat cuffs. I just, I haven't taken the time for me. And everybody was surprised. And I ended up doing a world tour and start traveling everywhere. And I ended up in Bangkok. I realized, wow, I like the sovereignty. I want to do my own thing. I'm going to found my own business. I started talking to a buddy that was at DocuSign. We started working together. And naturally we got into advising and got into consulting work. And then realized I'm doing more than just consulting and advising. I'm in the trenches. So naturally it starts turning into fractional work, right? As my co-founder and I are building a software business and then we're taking on three, four, five different clients, I'm realizing that, wow, like this is a little difficult managing my time versus the client time. And there's fires that pop up. And my co-founder is very process oriented and so am I. And we started running all of our processes through Agile and started using different frameworks. And then we're realizing, well, there's just a lot to this to be able to manage your time the best way possible. And so it kind of just led us where we're here. We had some insights on some stuff that we're seeing out in the world. We've been talking to a whole bunch of fractionals recently and we're just trying to figure out like, are we biased? Are we hearing the same issues that everyone else is hearing? Are you as a fractional dealing with this? And that's kind of what kind of led me to this call with you just to get a better understanding is like if this core insight is true, it leads us to our hypothesis for a product that we're working on. And the end goal of this is like, hopefully today I can show you some different problems that we're seeing out there. And maybe you can confirm if these are things that you're dealing with as well. And then what I can tell you-

CHRISLEY: What was that?

TABARAK: I said, here's what I can tell you just from my POV speaking with other fractionals and my side ventures that I have going on.

chunks.speaker: interviewee
chunks.raw_text: CHRISLEY: The high majority of fractionals that I come across don't have enough work to be in a position where they need help with their time. Their biggest pain point that I see is getting people to adhere is the wrong word, but think of fractional work as a real alternative. And I'm specifically referring to more of your fractional CXO positions or your fractional C-suite positions where they either view it as, they almost view it as a step trial. And I get it from one standpoint, it's like, hey, we want people who are fully committed and not necessarily just doing this part time. And so what, at least from what I get from other fractionals is their biggest pain point is getting clients and not, especially if they're not a fractional CMO or fractional like CRO where their skillset isn't necessarily sales, it's selling themselves. Now, with what you're stating, I do think once there is a bucket of fractionals that do have the pain point that you're expressing, I just think it's going to be that much more difficult for you to isolate those fractionals from the fractionals that have the difficulties of just getting the clients. Like, I don't know how you would filter out between those because it's not to say that I think what you're doing is incorrect. I do think there are fractionals that have that pain point that you're expressing. I just don't know how you would differentiate to finding them versus the fractionals where the high majority of them just don't know how to get clients.

chunks.speaker: interviewer
chunks.raw_text: TABARAK: You know what's weird? I love that you said that because I literally had an insight in a recent conversation and I didn't even update my slide fully and me and my co-founder actually just talked about the solution you're talking about, the issue you're talking about, right? The number one problem, this is the number one problem we're hearing. It does not matter who we're talking to. From the most skilled, somebody who's been there for a long time, it's the feast or famine cycle, right? It's when you're working, you're working, you're doing all this stuff, you're taking care of all your clients and then the next thing you know, you look up and your engagement is done and you have no more new clients in the pipeline, right? Yeah, that's the biggest concern that they have. So here's my question for you, right? Because we found this and I think our solution that we have is, and we'll go through it a little bit more, but it's the core premise that everything builds on top of. And so if you don't do the thing that we're, I mean, especially I came from sales, right? I mean, this is 100% a sales thing, right? I have had the best sales representatives. I have been where you're successful, you're killing it, you're getting all the revenue, you're closing all the deals and then you're not making anything, right? And I would have reps reach out to me and they'd be like, I don't know what's going on. I'm doing the same thing I'm doing every day. You know, last month when I had all those deals, I'm doing the same thing. And the reality is, is no, you're not, right? And the core thing that I realized for me and then maybe for my sales reps back in the day was that there's a portion of time that has to be allocated to getting your customers, going to go find your clients, right? And what I'm trying to discover, I guess, from this insight from what you just brought up right now, which is thank you so much, you like confirmed our thing is, it sounds like it's two angles. One angle is you're not consistently prospecting or keeping that, you know, like client fires are happening, life is happening, you're getting really crazy and you're putting that on the back burner. Or the second alternative is, oh, you are spending two hours every day in the trenches, you're on LinkedIn, you're doing whatever and you just still cannot get clients. So your marketing skill is just not progressing or getting better, even though you're putting two hours in a day. And so there's one of those problems we can solve for, the other one is kind of we're trying to stay out of, but what we're really trying to find out inherently is like, if I were to ask you like, hey, last week, how many hours did you spend each day in prospecting for new clients? What would you call that amount of time?

Like that's, if I'm not doing client work, I'm doing that, you know what I mean? Like, so in terms of hours, sheesh, I don't know, maybe three, three, four hours a day I spend doing that personally. But I think the high majority of fractionals, especially because again, most fractionals are in some sort of C-suite range, they're not doing that. And a lot of that is ego, if anything else, like it's like, bro, I don't wanna be spending time doing all this crap. And I get it, 100% get it.

Especially because everybody and their grandmother is now doing LinkedIn automations and email automations. And then you would just have to get that windfall and make that consistent muscle. Do you think it's a, I

chunks.speaker: interviewee
chunks.raw_text: CHRISLEY: I think not. Yeah. And the reason why I think not is one, the high majority of them don't come from sales. That's first and foremost. So because they don't come from sales, they're attempting to sell themselves in these messages. Where if we look at LinkedIn, if we look at email for perspective, everybody's inbox is flooded with cold emails, especially if they have a business domain. Like, so there's that. Then because cold email historically has always been, hey, I'm Chris, I'm from Google. I help companies search for whatever they need. Do you wanna do a 15 minute demo or something? That style of cold email doesn't work, but the industry or the market hasn't caught up to the fact that that style of email doesn't work. And so the reason why I say no is because I don't think people have realized, bro, your emails are not differentiating you at all from the noise. Your outreach is not differentiating you. Regardless of if it's through email or LinkedIn, you're trying to sell your service through the cold outreach. Where the cold outreach, what you should be trying to sell is a conversation. You should be trying to sell talking to me.

chunks.speaker: interviewee
chunks.raw_text: CHRISLEY: And talking to me is going to be, like now granted, great. If someone is willing to buy your service from cold outreach, like that's the anomaly. But that should not be what you're trying to scale and repeat it. You should be trying to scale conversations and you should be selling conversations. You should not be selling. And so ultimately you're trying to sell a relationship. And I think many fractionals, because of now AI and everything, they're automating stuff. AI is putting together scripts that is, again, trying to sell the product or service. And rightfully so. But that's not what's going to work from an outbound perspective. Now referrals inbound, that's a different ball of wax. But from a pure outbound perspective, you need to be selling your credibility. You essentially need to be peacocking. Like, hey, I'm the shit. Here's why I'm the shit. Irregardless of if you work with me or not, you spending 30 minutes with me is going to be a valuable time spent, if you will.

chunks.speaker: interviewee
chunks.raw_text: CHRISLEY: And I don't think fractionals are in that mind space. I don't think businesses are in that mind space, quite frankly. But in that approach, they're not realizing there's still human, how do I say this? Human interaction has not changed. The method in which we do things have changed, but it's still, I'm buying from you. Your product or service is one thing, but I'm buying from you because I rock with you. And if you're not trying to build, if you're not generating conversations to build that rapport, you're already shooting yourself in the head.

chunks.speaker: interviewer

Your website has to be optimized in that same manner. You need to have consistency with your blog posts or whatever it is that is bolstering said credibility.
